story:
A gigantic bone jutted up from black goo, beckoning from beneath the surface. In the 1800s, Anya—the Storyteller of the Blue Lantern—felt her breath catch and her heart pound. The legends were true. She could almost hear the underworld murmur inside the glistening black seep, neither liquid nor solid, as if alive.

“Stop paddling. Don’t get too close,” she warned from the reed boat. “It will take you.”

Around them, a dry, broad plain opened its arms. Distant mountains peeked through morning mists. Sage stung the air with a clean, sharp spice that drifted into minds and stirred memories older than time. Images rose in Anya as if inherited: her grandmother’s tales of ancient beasts—saber-toothed cats, towering, hairy creatures with long tusks, giant bears, slow giants with claws. Long ago, when ice cloaked the earth—eleven thousand, fifty thousand, maybe one hundred thousand years past—creatures larger than ships had wandered here. Some legends even whispered of monstrous reptiles. Lured to dark pools seeping from the earth, they stepped into the sticky depths and were held beneath the midnight sludge.

It was Anya’s first journey inland by river from the ocean at a place called Los Angeles. Their ship, Destiny’s Dreams, a wooden three-masted vessel, bobbed at anchor in the bay. In the small Peruvian reed boat—“little reed horse,” its bow shaped like a cougar’s head and its stern like a bear—Anya, Captain Alfonse, Adelberto the First Mate, Isabella his wife, the woman known as Wind Reader, Akela from Hawai‘i, Luciana the mapmaker, and a few sailors drifted near a string of black ponds that winked like oil-black mirrors.

On his world voyages, Captain Alfonse collected ancient arts and wisdom. The first time he saw a reed boat from the great Andean lake, a shiver ran through him. He brought one aboard Destiny’s Dreams to paddle into shallow places and narrow waterways like this one.

“Let’s see the bone of a giant creature,” a young sailor blurted. Before Anya’s warning could stop him, fifteen-year-old Akela—a brave soul still learning a leader’s caution—leaped ashore. He stepped onto murky ground, reached toward the beckoning bone, and the earth answered with a sticky shlup. His leg dropped to the ankle. He gasped, arms pinwheeling.

“Freeze,” Anya commanded, voice cutting the air. “Don’t move. You’ll sink.”

Adelberto scrambled onto a rocky ledge and thrust a branch toward Akela. “Grab it!”

Akela stretched. The tarry surface gurgled and swallowed him deeper. When Adelberto edged closer, the ooze surged to claim his foot, too.

“I think I know what to do,” Anya said. She seized a coil of rope. Alfonse’s eyes met hers: yes. “Everyone—paddles ready. Paddle like lives depend on it.”

She hurled the line. Adelberto caught it; Akela wrapped it around his arm. “Pull!” Anya and Alfonse shouted. The crew dug paddles into the river, driving the boat’s weight against the line. The black brew drew a long, reluctant breath—then let go. Akela fell into Adelberto’s arms. Captain Alfonse hauled them aboard, slick with tar and trembling but alive.

That night, in slanted script, Anya wrote it all in her journal.

Decades later, the faded journals from Destiny’s Dreams were found in a sailor’s trunk in the attic of the Mystical Coast Lighthouse keeper’s house. On a rainy day, a ten-year-old named Emily read this story aloud to her friends. A blue glass lantern nearby caught the thin window light, and indigo shadows climbed the walls. In the wavering shapes, they saw a square-sailed ship, a reed boat with a cougar’s bow and a bear’s stern, mountains in mist, and figures leaning into their paddles. “Look,” one child whispered, pointing. It was as if memory itself had slipped free of the pages and painted the room with living stories.
